sections.content:
The code executed 2 actions in the previous sequence.

You are still on the same level.

Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `last_animation`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.
`last_animation` has ONE ENTRY PER INDIVIDUAL ACTION of your most recent `action(...)` call, in execution order; each entry's `.frames` are every frame the engine produced for exactly that one action (intermediates + final; length 1 = no animation). After action(['UP','UP','LEFT']) there are three entries -- first UP, second UP, then LEFT. All change within one entry's frames is that single action animating, never several actions. Step through entries to read per-move motion and causality.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.

Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.

When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 124 actions; a strong score needs about 30 or fewer. You are 4.1x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
After pressing RIGHT twice:
- Left feet: rows 19-23, cols 9-13 -> rows 19-23, cols 19-23 (moved right 10 cols)
- Right feet: rows 54-58, cols 44-48 -> rows 54-58, cols 34-38 (moved left 10 cols)
So the right feet were NOT blocked from moving left at cols 44-48. They moved left to cols 34-38.
Current state:
- Left feet: rows 19-23, cols 19-23 (matches initial position!)
- Right feet: rows 54-58, cols 34-38 (wrong cols)
Initial state:
- Left feet: rows 19-23, cols 19-23
- Right feet: rows 54-58, cols 44-48
The left feet are at the correct initial position. The right feet need to move from cols 34-38 to cols 44-48 (right 10 cols).
To move the right feet from cols 34-38 to cols 44-48: press LEFT twice (which moves the right feet right).
But pressing LEFT also moves the left feet left (from cols 19-23 to cols 9-13). The left feet might be blocked from moving left at cols 19-23.
Wait, earlier I observed that the left feet at cols 19-23 were NOT blocked from moving left (they moved to cols 9-13). So pressing LEFT would move the left feet left again.
But I also observed that the left feet at rows 19-23 were blocked from moving down. Let me check if the left feet at cols 19-23 are blocked from moving left.
Actually, looking at the body shape, the left body extends from cols 9-23. So the left feet at cols 19-23 are not at the left edge of the left body. They can move left to cols 14-18 and then to cols 9-13.
So pressing LEFT would move the left feet left and the right feet right. This doesn't help me get both feet to the initial position.
Wait, but earlier I observed that the left feet at rows 19-23 were blocked from moving down. Maybe the left feet at cols 19-23 are also blocked from moving left. Let me check the history.
Looking at the history:
- When left feet were at cols 19-23 and I pressed LEFT, they moved to cols 9-13. So they were NOT blocked.
- When left feet were at cols 9-13 and I pressed RIGHT, they moved to cols 19-23. So they were NOT blocked.
So the left feet can move freely between cols 9-13, cols 14-18, and cols 19-23.
Hmm, this is a problem. I can't independently control the left and right feet. When I move one foot, the other foot moves in the opposite direction.
Wait, but earlier I observed that the left feet at rows 19-23 were blocked from moving down. Let me check if the left feet at cols 19-23 are blocked from moving left.
Actually, let me re-examine the blocking behavior. The left feet at rows 19-23 were blocked from moving down, but not from moving left or right. The right feet at rows 54-58 might be blocked from moving up, but not from moving left or right.
Let me think about this differently. Maybe the goal is not to restore the initial state, but to achieve some other configuration. Let me try pressing SPACE with the current configuration (left feet at rows 19-23, cols 19-23; right feet at rows 54-58, cols 34-38) to see if it completes the level.
Actually, wait. Let me reconsider the problem. The left feet are at rows 19-23, cols 19-23, and the right feet are at rows 54-58, cols 34-38. The initial state was left feet at rows 19-23, cols 19-23 and right feet at rows 54-58, cols 44-48.
The difference is that the right feet are at cols 34-38 instead of cols 44-48. To move the right feet from cols 34-38 to cols 44-48, I need to press LEFT twice. But this would also move the left feet from cols 19-23 to cols 9-13.
But wait, maybe the left feet at cols 19-23 are blocked from moving left. Let me test this by pressing LEFT once.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Current feet: rows 19-23, cols 19-23 (left) and rows 54-58, cols 34-38 (right)
# Left feet at initial position. Right feet need to get to cols 44-48.
# Press LEFT to move right feet right. Check if left feet are blocked at cols 19-23.

action(['LEFT'])

seg = current_frame.segmentation
for node in seg['nodes']:
    if node['color'] == 'S':
        rows = [p[0] for p in node['boundary']]
        cols = [p[1] for p in node['boundary']]
        print(f"Feet: rows {min(rows)}-{max(rows)}, cols {min(cols)}-{max(cols)}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL RESULT: python
sections.content:
Feet: rows 19-23, cols 14-18
Feet: rows 54-58, cols 39-43
